era_context: Late Bronze Age, mid-18th Dynasty Egypt under Thutmose III or his immediate successor. The New Kingdom was at its height. The Mitanni kingdom controlled northern Mesopotamia and Syria. The Hittite Empire was consolidating power in Anatolia. Mycenaean Greece was in the Late Helladic II period. This era is characterized by intensive military campaigns, administrative consolidation, and the development of international trade networks.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Shang Dynasty records exist but are sparse and chronologically uncertain)

events.title: Thutmose III's continued military campaigns in the Levant
events.description: Thutmose III continued his systematic military campaigns to consolidate Egyptian control over Levantine territories. These campaigns were part of a long-term strategy to establish Egyptian hegemony and secure tribute networks. The campaigns are documented in temple inscriptions and administrative records.

sources.name: Karnak Temple inscriptions (Annals of Thutmose III)

events.certainty_note: Thutmose III's reign is well-attested, but specific year-by-year events are difficult to pinpoint. This entry reflects the general pattern of his campaigns.

events.title: Hittite expansion in Anatolia
events.description: The Hittite Empire continued to expand its control over Anatolian territories during this period. Military campaigns focused on securing vassal relationships and establishing administrative control. The expansion laid the groundwork for later imperial consolidation.
key_figures: Hittite Great King (identity uncertain due to chronological debate)
events.certainty_note: Hittite chronology is debated; the identity of the ruler in 1407 BCE is uncertain. This entry reflects the general pattern of Hittite expansion during the mid-15th century BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: As with previous years, the chronology of this period is contested. The 'low chronology' would shift these events significantly later. No specific events are individually attested to 1407 BCE in any contemporary source. The entries above represent the general historical context rather than year-specific documentation.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1407 BCE is similar to previous years: dependent on Egyptian temple inscriptions and cuneiform records, with significant chronological uncertainty. The surviving sources are biased toward elite political and military history. Archaeological evidence provides some constraints but cannot pinpoint individual years. The lack of contemporary cross-dating between Egyptian, Mesopotamian, and Hittite records for this specific year makes precise synchronization difficult.

graph_edges.note: Part of the same campaign sequence
